title: Export Horticulture Production Guide for Zimbabwe - Fine Beans, Snow Peas, Blueberries

metadata.source: Zimbabwe Horticultural Promotion Council / AGRITEX / GlobalGAP

content:
EXPORT HORTICULTURE IN ZIMBABWE

Zimbabwe is a significant exporter of fresh produce to the European Union and United Kingdom. The country's climatic advantages (counter-season production, altitude, sunshine) make it competitive in high-value fresh produce. Key export crops include fine beans (haricot verts), snow peas/mange tout, sugar snap peas, blueberries, and cut flowers.

GLOBALGAP CERTIFICATION:
All export produce MUST be GlobalGAP certified. This requires:
- Documented food safety management system
- Traceability from seed to pack house
- Integrated Pest Management (IPM) programme
- Pesticide residue testing (comply with EU Maximum Residue Limits)
- Worker health, safety, and welfare standards (GRASP module)
- Environmental management and water stewardship
- Annual audit by accredited certification body

PESTICIDE MANAGEMENT FOR EXPORT:
- ONLY use pesticides registered in Zimbabwe AND approved in the destination market
- Check EU Pesticide Database for approved active ingredients and MRLs
- Maintain spray records: product, rate, date, operator, PHI (Pre-Harvest Interval)
- Observe PHIs strictly - residue violations result in rejection and loss of market access
- Prefer biological controls (Bt, Spinosad, predatory insects) where possible
- Rotate chemical classes to prevent resistance buildup

COLD CHAIN MANAGEMENT:
Export produce is perishable and requires unbroken cold chain:
1. Field cooling: harvest in cool morning hours, move to shade immediately
2. Pack house: pre-cool to target temperature within 1-2 hours
3. Cold storage: hold at crop-specific temperature (0-4C for most)
4. Transport: refrigerated trucks to airport
5. Air freight: temperature-controlled cargo hold
6. Target: farm to UK/EU retailer shelf in 48-72 hours

FINE BEANS (HARICOT VERTS):
Zimbabwe's #1 horticultural export. Varieties: Samantha, Amy (extra-fine), Bronco (bobby).
- Season: Year-round with irrigation. Peak demand March-October.
- Quality: Pods must be straight, uniform, dark green, stringless, <8mm diameter (fine) or <6mm (extra-fine).
- Pack: 1.5kg or 4kg cartons. Barcoded with lot/field traceability.
- Key challenge: Bean Fly in first 3 weeks - Imidacloprid seed dressing essential.
- Pick every 2 days maximum.

SNOW PEAS / MANGE TOUT:
Flat-podded pea eaten whole. Major export alongside fine beans.
- Season: March-July (cool season only). Stagger plantings every 2-3 weeks.
- Quality: Flat, translucent pods with barely visible peas. Must be straight and snap cleanly.
- Trellising essential (1.5-2m support).
- Very temperature sensitive - will not produce above 25C.
- Pick DAILY during peak production.

SUGAR SNAP PEAS:
Round-podded pea eaten whole with developed peas inside.
- Growing demand. Commands premium price over snow peas.
- Same production system as snow peas but harvest when pods are plump.

BLUEBERRIES:
Rapidly growing export industry. Zimbabwe produces during Northern Hemisphere off-season (Aug-Dec).
- Establishment: USD 15,000-25,000/ha. Payback in 3-4 years.
- Requires acid soil (pH 4.5-5.5) - most Zimbabwe soils need acidification.
- Drip irrigation essential. Pine bark mulch 10-15cm.
- NEVER use Compound D or lime - kills plants.
- Harvest season: September-December. Premium prices USD 8-18/kg.
- Cold chain critical: pre-cool to 0-2C within 2 hours. Air-freight within 48 hours.
- Varieties: Legacy, Ventura, Eureka Sunrise (all low-chill southern highbush types).

KEY EXPORTERS IN ZIMBABWE:
- Nhimbe Fresh, Interfresh, Selby Enterprises, York Farm, Kondozi
- Zimbabwe Horticultural Promotion Council coordinates industry
- Horticulture contributes USD 80-120 million annually to Zimbabwe's economy

title: Pea and Green Bean Production for Zimbabwe - Garden Peas, Snow Peas, Fine Beans
content:
PEA AND GREEN BEAN PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

GARDEN PEAS (Pisum sativum):
Grown for shelling - peas removed from pod for fresh eating or freezing.

Planting: March-June (cool season). Direct seed at 5-7cm spacing in rows 30cm apart. Seed rate 80-120 kg/ha. Depth 3-5cm.
Soil: Well-drained loams, pH 6.0-7.0. Inoculate with Rhizobium leguminosarum.
Temperature: Optimal 15-20C. Will NOT set pods above 27C. Cool nights essential.
Fertilizer: Compound D 200-300 kg/ha basal. Peas fix nitrogen.
Water: 25mm/week. Shallow-rooted - drought sensitive.
Pests: Pea Aphid, Pod Borer, Cutworm. Powdery Mildew is #1 disease - spray Sulphur preventatively.
Harvest: Pick every 2-3 days when pods well-filled but still green. Test - peas should be tender.
Post-harvest: Highly perishable. Cool immediately. Shell and freeze for storage.

SNOW PEAS / MANGE TOUT (eaten-pod types):
Entire pod consumed before peas develop. Premium export crop.

Varieties: Oregon Sugar Pod (flat/mange tout), Sugar Snap (round/snap type).
Planting: March-July. Stagger every 2-3 weeks for continuous supply.
Spacing: 75cm rows, 5-7cm in-row. Support with 1.5-2m trellis - ESSENTIAL.
Quality: Export pods must be flat (snow pea) or plump (sugar snap), straight, bright green, no blemishes.
PHI compliance: Only use EU-approved pesticides. Keep records.
Harvest: DAILY for export quality. Grade in field.
Cold chain: Cool to 2-4C within 1 hour. Pack in approved cartons.

GREEN BEANS / FINE BEANS (Phaseolus vulgaris):
Zimbabwe's most important horticultural export crop.

Types:
- Extra-fine (haricot vert): <6mm diameter. Highest price. Variety: Amy.
- Fine: 6-8mm diameter. Main export grade. Variety: Samantha.
- Bobby/Standard: 8-12mm. Local market and processing. Variety: Bronco.

Planting: Year-round with irrigation. Direct seed. 45cm rows, 5-7cm in-row.
CRITICAL: Bean Fly is devastating in first 3 weeks. Imidacloprid seed dressing non-negotiable.
Fertilizer: 2:3:4 at 400-600 kg/ha basal. Foliar calcium and boron for pod quality.
Irrigation: Drip essential for export. Consistent moisture prevents curved pods.
Harvest: Every 2 days for fine beans. Pods must be straight, uniform, correct diameter.
Grading: By length (10-13cm) and diameter (<6mm extra-fine, 6-8mm fine).
Pack: 1.5kg or 4kg approved cartons with barcode traceability.

ROTATION:
- Rotate peas and beans with non-legume crops (cereals, brassicas)
- Do not plant peas/beans in same field more than 2 consecutive seasons
- Allow 3-year break to reduce soil-borne diseases (Fusarium, Sclerotinia)

title: Tea, Sesame, Cassava and Other Speciality Crops in Zimbabwe
content:
SPECIALITY CROPS IN ZIMBABWE

TEA (Camellia sinensis):
Grown in Zimbabwe's Eastern Highlands (Honde Valley, Chipinge, Nyanga, Chimanimani) at 900-2000m altitude with >1200mm rainfall.

Industry: Zimbabwe produces ~24,000 tonnes annually. Tanganda Tea Company is largest producer. Significant smallholder outgrower schemes.
Varieties: SFS 150 (high yield), PC 108 (high quality), and various clonal selections.
Establishment: Plant rooted cuttings Nov-Feb at 9,000-13,000 plants/ha.
Soil: Deep acid soils pH 4.5-5.5. Red loams ideal.
Fertilizer: 200-400 kg N/ha/year (Ammonium Sulphate to maintain acidity). Split 4-6 applications.
Plucking: 'Two leaves and a bud' every 7-14 days. Quality depends on fine plucking.
Processing: CTC (Crush-Tear-Curl) method for black tea. Wither -> CTC -> Oxidise -> Dry -> Sort.
Timeline: No harvest years 1-3. Light plucking year 4. Full production year 6+.
Economics: Long productive life (50-100+ years). High establishment cost but steady returns.

SESAME (Sesamum indicum):
Growing export crop suited to hot, dry lowveld and middleveld regions.

Planting: December-January (needs warm soil >25C). Direct seed 3-5 kg/ha mixed with sand.
Spacing: 45cm rows, 10-15cm in-row. Depth: 1-2cm maximum (very small seed).
Soil: Well-drained sandy soils, pH 5.5-7.0. Cannot tolerate waterlogging.
Fertilizer: Low-input. Compound D 100-200 kg/ha if available.
Harvest: CRITICAL TIMING - harvest when lower capsules brown but before shattering. Cut, stack upright, shake onto tarpaulin.
Drying: <6% moisture for export grade.
Market: White sesame commands USD 1,200-2,000/tonne. Growing demand from Asia, Middle East, EU.

CASSAVA (Manihot esculenta):
Important food security crop in northern Zimbabwe (Muzarabani, Guruve, Mt Darwin, Kariba, Zambezi Valley).

Planting: Oct-Dec. Stem cuttings 20-30cm on mounds/ridges. 10,000 plants/ha.
Soil: Extremely adaptable - grows on poor sandy soils pH 4.5-7.5.
Drought tolerance: Extremely high once established.
Varieties: Sauti, Nyalanda (both CMD-resistant, sweet/low-cyanide types).
Harvest: From 9 months onwards. Piecemeal harvest - leave roots in ground as 'living storage' for up to 24 months.
Processing: PERISHABLE fresh - process within 48 hours. Peel, chip, dry into flour.
Food security: Ultimate survival crop - produces calories on marginal land in drought years.

GARLIC (Allium sativum):
High-value import substitution crop. Zimbabwe imports significant garlic.

Planting: March-May (cool season). Individual cloves, pointed end up, 3-5cm deep.
Spacing: 20cm rows, 10-12cm in-row. 800-1200 kg cloves/ha.
Soil: Well-drained, fertile, pH 6.0-7.0. NEVER follow onion/garlic (White Rot risk).
Fertilizer: 2:3:4 at 600-800 kg/ha basal.
WATCH OUT: White Rot (Sclerotium cepivorum) - soil-borne, persists 20+ years. No cure. Prevention only.
Harvest: When 50-60% leaves dried. Cure 2-4 weeks.
Economics: USD 3-8/kg retail. Excellent returns from small area.

STRAWBERRIES (Fragaria x ananassa):
Growing premium market around Harare, Marondera, Nyanga.

Varieties: Festival, Chandler (short-day types).
Planting: March-April for May-November production.
System: Raised beds, plastic mulch, drip irrigation. Tunnel/greenhouse for premium quality.
Soil: Sandy loams pH 5.5-6.5. Fumigated/solarised for disease control.
Pests: Red Spider Mite (biological control with Phytoseiulus), Botrytis (ventilation + fungicides).
Harvest: Every 2-3 days when 3/4 red. Handle gently.
Market: Premium domestic USD 5-10/kg. Hotels, restaurants, supermarkets.

GREEN PEPPER / SWEET PEPPER (Capsicum annuum):
Widely grown for fresh market. Transplant Sept-Nov from July-Aug nursery.
Spacing: 75cm x 40-50cm. Stake plants when fruit-laden.
Fertilizer: Compound S 400-600 kg/ha basal. Calcium Nitrate to prevent Blossom End Rot.
Harvest: Green at full size, or leave to ripen red/yellow for premium.
